turns.text: Hey, so I'm a broke college student learning to cook lol. Two things though: always give measurements in metric because cups confuse me, and please always suggest a cheaper substitution if an ingredient is expensive.
Got it! I'll use metric measurements and always suggest a cheaper substitution when possible.
I have a tiny kitchen. Literally just one burner, a small pot, a pan, and a knife. Is that enough to actually cook decent food?

That setup is honestly perfect for student cooking. With one pot and one pan you can make pasta, stir fries, eggs, fried rice, soups, oatmeal, and a lot of one-pot meals. The trick is choosing simple recipes and ingredients that overlap so you don't need a full kitchen.
Okay good because my budget is also tiny. What are some cheap staples I should keep around?

Budget cooking is basically about versatile staples: rice, pasta, potatoes, onions, garlic, eggs, canned tomatoes, frozen vegetables, and a cheap protein like lentils or beans. Those ingredients can combine into dozens of meals. Frozen vegetables are especially good because they last forever and are usually cheaper than fresh.
I tried cutting onions yesterday and it looked like a disaster. Any basic knife tips?

Start slow and focus on control rather than speed. Keep your fingertips tucked in slightly so the knife touches your knuckles instead of your fingertips. For onions: cut it in half, peel it, keep the root side intact, make vertical slices, then cut across. You'll suddenly get nice little cubes instead of random chunks.
Nice. Also I waste food a lot because things go bad. Any storage tips?

Store ingredients based on how fast they spoil. Onions, potatoes, and garlic stay fine in a cool dark place. Cooked food should go into sealed containers in the fridge and usually lasts about 3–4 days. If you cook a big batch, portion it and freeze some immediately so it doesn't go bad.
What about breakfast? I always end up skipping it.

Fast student breakfasts: oatmeal, eggs on toast, yogurt with fruit, or banana peanut butter sandwiches. Oatmeal is especially cheap and takes about 5 minutes in a pot. You can even add leftover fruit or nuts to make it more filling.
Snacks though... that's where I spend money. Any cheap snack ideas?

Try roasted chickpeas, popcorn in a pot, toast with peanut butter, or boiled eggs. Popcorn kernels are insanely cheap and one bag lasts forever. Just heat a little oil in a pot, add kernels, cover it, and shake occasionally.
Checkpoint time: can you give me a really cheap pasta recipe?

Budget garlic tomato pasta:

Ingredients:
- 200 g dry pasta
- 15 ml oil
- 3 cloves garlic
- 200 g canned tomatoes
- Salt and pepper
- Optional: 20 g grated cheese

Steps:
1. Boil pasta in salted water until tender.
2. In a pan heat the oil and cook chopped garlic for about 30 seconds.
3. Add canned tomatoes and simmer for 5 minutes.
4. Mix the cooked pasta with the sauce and season with salt and pepper.
5. Add cheese on top if you have it.

Cheap substitution: if canned tomatoes are expensive, use 1–2 chopped fresh tomatoes or even a spoon of tomato paste mixed with 100 ml water.
That actually sounds doable. Can I add protein somehow?

Definitely. The cheapest additions are lentils, beans, or eggs. For example, add 100 g cooked lentils into the sauce while it simmers. Another trick is cracking an egg into hot pasta and stirring quickly so it coats the noodles.
I've heard meal prep saves money but it sounds boring.

Meal prep doesn't have to mean eating the exact same thing daily. Cook a base like rice or pasta, then change toppings each day. One day beans and salsa, another day stir-fried vegetables, another day eggs. Same base, different flavor.
Speaking of stir fry, I always burn it.

High heat is good, but overcrowding the pan causes burning and steaming. Cook vegetables in small batches and keep them moving. Start with harder vegetables like carrots, then softer ones like cabbage or peppers.
Frozen vegetables okay for stir fry?
Absolutely. Frozen vegetables are often cheaper and already chopped. Just add them straight into the hot pan and cook until the water evaporates and they start to brown slightly.
What about rice? Mine always ends up weird.

Use a simple ratio: about 200 g rice to 400 ml water. Bring it to a boil, reduce heat, cover, and cook about 12 minutes. Then turn off heat and let it sit covered for another 5 minutes before fluffing.
Is fried rice a good leftover meal?
Perfect leftover meal. Day-old rice is actually better because it's drier. Just fry garlic, add rice, toss in vegetables, and scramble an egg into it.
Checkpoint again: give me a super simple stir fry recipe.

Basic student stir fry:

Ingredients:
- 200 g cooked rice
- 150 g frozen mixed vegetables
- 1 egg
- 10 ml oil
- 15 ml soy sauce

Steps:
1. Heat oil in a pan.
2. Add frozen vegetables and cook 3–4 minutes.
3. Add rice and stir until heated through.
4. Push rice to one side and scramble the egg.
5. Mix everything together and add soy sauce.

Cheap substitution: if soy sauce is expensive, use a pinch of salt with a little vinegar or chili sauce for flavor.
Nice. How do I stop rice from sticking to the pan?
Use enough oil and make sure the pan is already hot before adding the rice. Also break up the rice with your hands before cooking so it isn't clumped together.
What are good one-pot meals? Washing dishes is my enemy.
One-pot classics: lentil soup, tomato pasta, rice and beans, vegetable curry, or chili. These meals cook everything together so flavors build while dishes stay minimal.
Lentils sound healthy but I never used them.
They’re one of the best budget proteins. Red lentils cook in about 15 minutes and turn creamy, great for soups or curries. Brown lentils hold their shape and work well in rice dishes.
How long do cooked meals last in the fridge usually?
Most cooked meals stay safe about 3–4 days if sealed well. Rice should ideally be eaten within 2–3 days. If you're unsure you'll finish it, freeze a portion right away.
Freezing food actually works well?
Yes. Soups, sauces, rice, beans, and pasta sauces freeze very well. Portion them into small containers so you can reheat only what you need.
What's the cheapest protein overall?
Eggs, lentils, beans, and chickpeas are usually the cheapest protein sources. They’re also very versatile and store well.
Okay random question: how do restaurants get vegetables so evenly chopped?

Consistency comes from using a stable cutting shape first. For example, slice a carrot into long slabs, stack them, then cut into sticks, then cubes. The key idea is turning round vegetables into flat shapes before chopping.
I feel like I'm slowly becoming less terrible at cooking.

That's exactly how it works. Cooking skill grows through repetition more than complicated recipes. Once you know a few techniques—boiling pasta, frying eggs, sautéing vegetables—you can combine them endlessly.
Alright last big one coming up.
Ready for it.
Checkpoint: give me a one-pot meal I can cook for like three days of food.

Simple lentil tomato stew:

Ingredients:
- 200 g red lentils
- 400 g canned tomatoes
- 1 onion (about 120 g)
- 2 cloves garlic
- 15 ml oil
- 600 ml water
- Salt and pepper

Steps:
1. Heat oil in a pot and cook chopped onion for 3–4 minutes.
2. Add garlic and cook 30 seconds.
3. Add lentils, canned tomatoes, and water.
4. Simmer about 15–20 minutes until lentils soften.
5. Season with salt and pepper.

Cheap substitution: if canned tomatoes are expensive, replace them with 300 g chopped fresh tomatoes or mix 1 tablespoon tomato paste with 300 ml water.
That sounds like exactly the kind of thing I need.
It reheats really well too, which makes it great for meal prep. You can also add rice or bread to stretch it even further.
What if I get bored of leftovers?
Turn leftovers into something new. Lentil stew can become a sauce for pasta, a filling for wraps, or a topping for rice. Same food, different format.
Cooking is starting to feel less intimidating honestly.
That's the magic of simple cooking. Once you realize most meals are just a combination of starch, vegetables, and protein, it becomes more like assembling building blocks.
Next step is probably learning spices right?
Exactly. A few cheap spices like chili flakes, cumin, paprika, and black pepper can completely change the personality of a dish without adding much cost.
Alright I'm actually excited to cook tonight now.
Mission accomplished. With a pot, a pan, and a few cheap ingredients you can already cook a surprising variety of meals.
